content:
# 2 Peter Chapter 1 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), page 269 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2PE_001_page_001.jpg

1. Symeon Petros, a slave and apostle of Yeshua Christos, to the [ones] having obtained an equally precious faith with us in [the] righteousness of the God of us and Savior Yeshua Christos:

2. Grace to you and peace be multiplied in [the] knowledge of God and of Yeshua the Lord of us.

3. As all [things] of the divine power of him — the [things] pertaining to life and godliness — have been granted to us through the knowledge of the [one] having called us by [his] own glory and virtue,

4. through which the precious and greatest promises have been granted to us, that through these you might become sharers of [the] divine nature, having escaped the corruption in the world by desire.

5. And this same [thing] also, [with] all diligence having brought alongside, supply in the faith of you the virtue, and in the virtue the knowledge,

6. and in the knowledge the self-control, and in the self-control the endurance, and in the endurance the godliness,

7. and in the godliness the brotherly affection, and in the brotherly affection the love.

8. For these [things] belonging to you and abounding make [you] not idle nor unfruitful in the knowledge of the Lord of us Yeshua Christos.

9. For [the one] in whom these [things] are not present is blind, being shortsighted, having received forgetfulness of the cleansing of the former sins of him.

10. Therefore rather, brothers, be diligent to make the calling and election of you firm; for doing these [things] you shall never stumble.

11. For thus richly shall be supplied to you the entrance into the eternal kingdom of the Lord of us and Savior Yeshua Christos.

12. Therefore I shall be ready always to remind you concerning these [things], even though [you are] knowing [them] and having been established in the present truth.

13. And I consider [it] right, for as long as I am in this tent, to stir you up by a reminder,

14. knowing that swift is the putting off of the tent of me, even as also the Lord of us Yeshua Christos made clear to me.

15. And I shall be diligent also [that] at each [time] after the departure of me you are to have the remembrance of these [things].

16. For not having followed cleverly devised myths did we make known to you the power and coming of the Lord of us Yeshua Christos, but having become eyewitnesses of the majesty of that [one].

17. For having received from God [the] Father honor and glory, [when] such a voice was brought to him by the Majestic Glory: "This is the Son of me, the Beloved, in whom I was well pleased" —

18. and this voice we ourselves heard, having been brought from heaven, being with him in the holy mountain.

19. And we have the prophetic word [made] more firm, to which you do well [to be] paying attention, as to a lamp shining in a dark place, until [the] day dawns and [the] morning star rises in the hearts of you.

20. This first knowing, that every prophecy of Scripture does not come to be of [one's] own interpretation.

21. For not by [the] will of man was prophecy brought at any time, but being carried by [the] Holy Spirit, men spoke from God.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.1 -- "Symeon" (Symeon):** Sinaiticus reads CYMECON (Symeon), the Hebraic form of the name, rather than the Hellenized "Simon" (Simon) found in many later manuscripts. This archaic spelling is shared by a few early witnesses and is widely regarded as the harder reading, likely original.

2. **v.1 -- "of the God of us and Savior Yeshua Christos" (tou theou hemon kai soteros Iesou Christou):** The Granville Sharp construction (single article governing two nouns joined by kai) identifies "God" and "Savior" as the same person -- Yeshua Christos. Sinaiticus preserves this high Christological reading intact.

3. **v.2 -- "of God and of Yeshua the Lord of us":** Sinaiticus reads en epignosei tou theou kai Iesou tou kuriou hemon -- "in the knowledge of God and of Yeshua the Lord of us." Some manuscripts read "of God and of Yeshua Christos the Lord of us" or simply "of the Lord of us." The Sinaiticus reading distinguishes God and Yeshua as separate objects of knowledge with the repeated article.

4. **v.3 -- "by [his] own glory and virtue" (idia doxe kai arete):** Sinaiticus reads dia doxes kai aretes ("through glory and virtue") as the instrumental means of calling. Some witnesses read idia ("his own"). The dative form in Sinaiticus is instrumentally construed. Variant: some MSS read dia doxes kai aretes, others idia doxe kai arete.

5. **v.4 -- "greatest" (megista):** Sinaiticus reads megista ("greatest, very great") rather than megala ("great"). The superlative form is distinctive and intensifies the quality of God's promises.

6. **v.5 -- "having brought alongside" (pareisenegkantes):** The compound verb pareisphero means "to bring in alongside, to supply additionally." The participial construction indicates that diligence is the prerequisite for the virtue-chain that follows in vv.5-7.

7. **v.9 -- "having received forgetfulness" (lethen labon):** Sinaiticus reads lethen labon -- literally "having taken forgetfulness." The expression is a Hellenistic idiom for willful forgetting. The cleansing (katharismos) refers to baptismal purification from pre-conversion sins.

8. **v.10 -- "calling and election" (klesin kai eklogen):** Sinaiticus preserves the order klesin kai eklogen ("calling and election"), the theological sequence: God calls, then confirms the election. The verb bebaian poieisthai ("to make firm") places responsibility on the believer to confirm what God has initiated.

9. **v.15 -- "departure" (exodon):** Sinaiticus reads exodon -- literally "exodus," the same word used in Luke 9:31 for the death of Yeshua discussed at the Transfiguration. This verbal link to the Transfiguration account (vv.16-18) may be deliberate.

10. **v.17 -- "This is the Son of me, the Beloved, in whom I was well pleased":** Sinaiticus reads houtos estin ho huios mou ho agapetos eis hon ego eudokesa. This closely matches Matt 17:5 (the Transfiguration voice). Some MSS add "Listen to him" (akouete autou), but Sinaiticus does not include this addition. The aorist eudokesa ("I was well pleased") is preserved rather than the present tense of some witnesses.

11. **v.19 -- "morning star" (phosphoros):** Sinaiticus reads phosphoros -- literally "light-bearer," the Greek name for Venus as the dawn star. This is the only NT occurrence of this word. It signifies the eschatological dawning of full revelation in the believer's heart.

12. **v.20 -- "of [one's] own interpretation" (idias epiluseos):** Sinaiticus reads idias epiluseos -- "of its own unloosing/interpretation." The word epilusis occurs only here in the NT. Whether "its own" refers to the prophet's own interpretation or the reader's private interpretation is debated; the context of v.21 (prophets carried by the Spirit) favors the former: prophecy did not originate from the prophet's own understanding.

13. **v.21 -- "being carried by [the] Holy Spirit, men spoke from God":** Sinaiticus reads hupo pneumatos hagiou pheromenoi elalesen apo theou anthropoi -- "being carried by [the] Holy Spirit, spoke from God, men." The verb phero ("carry, bear along") depicts the Spirit as the driving force behind prophetic speech, with human agency subordinate. Some MSS read "holy men of God spoke" (hagioi theou anthropoi elalesen); Sinaiticus omits hagioi before anthropoi.

-- EMET, AI Translator
